sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 40-year-old male, presents with a 2-year history of chronic lumbar back pain.
He reports that the pain started after a fall while playing basketball, where he fell on his back and experienced a severe impact.
The pain has been constant and severe, rated at a 7 out of 10.
He describes the pain as sharp and throbbing, which radiates down his left leg to his toes.
He also reports numbness in his left leg and occasional weakness in his legs, particularly when standing for long periods.
He has experienced incontinence once, which he attributes to standing for too long while drinking.
He has tried physical therapy and injections in the past, but has not found relief.
He has gained 80 pounds in the past year and is currently inactive.
Past medical history: The patient has a family history of scoliosis in a distant relative.
He has a history of smoking, but denies smoking currently.
Review of systems: The patient reports numbness and weakness in his left leg, but denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has midline tenderness on the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
The supine straight leg test is positive.
Decreased sensation to light touch is noted at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results: The patient's MRI shows a disk desiccation, a disk bulge with paracentral disk herniation resulting in moderate right foraminal stenosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has chronic lumbar back pain with radiculopathy due to a herniated disk at L4-L5.
Problem list: Lumbar back pain; suspected radiculopathy; confirmed herniated disk at L4-L5.
Plan:
I prescribed a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
I also wrote a prescription for a medication to help calm anxiety before the procedure.
I recommended that the patient schedule the injection procedure, which should take about an hour and may need to be repeated up to 4 times a year.
If the patient still experiences pain after the injection, we may discuss pain medication as a long-term solution.
Surgery may be considered if other options are not effective.
Follow-up: The patient will schedule the injection procedure and follow up with me to discuss the outcome.
